evals.prompt:
Write an argumentative humanities essay in Chicago Notes-Bibliography style from these survived findings. Thesis topic: 'the role of the unreliable narrator in early modernist fiction'. Use numbered footnotes and a full Bibliography. Do NOT include a Method or Results section. Write the essay to humanities-essay.md.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_hum_1 | title: Early modernist novels increasingly foreground narrators whose judgments the text quietly undermines | dimension: literary-history | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/modernist-narration | author: Booth | year: 1961
- id: urn:mif:f_hum_2 | title: Free indirect discourse blurs the boundary between narratorial and character voice, enabling irony | dimension: narrative-theory | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/free-indirect | author: Cohn | year: 1978
- id: urn:mif:f_hum_3 | title: Readers reconstruct an implied author's values against the narrator's distortions | dimension: reception | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/implied-author | author: Rabinowitz | year: 1987
evals.expected_output: An argumentative essay with an Introduction stating a thesis, thematic argument sections (claim/evidence/interpretation), a Conclusion, and a Bibliography. Numbered footnotes (e.g., [^1]) carry the citations. No Method and no Results section. No raw urn:mif: identifiers in prose or footnotes.

deterministic_checks.description: Required Bibliography section present (not an APA References list)
At least one citation URL from the MIF findings appears in a footnote or the Bibliography
No raw urn:mif: identifiers appear in the prose or footnotes
No internal MIF finding IDs appear in the prose

expectations.description: The essay has NO Method and NO Results section
expectations.criteria: The essay does not contain a Method, Methodology, or Results section heading. Its structure is Introduction (with thesis), thematic argument sections, Conclusion, Bibliography — an argument, not an empirical report. This is the distinguishing feature versus the academic genre.

expectations.description: Citations are Chicago Notes-Bibliography, not author-date
expectations.criteria: Sources are documented in numbered footnotes/endnotes and a Bibliography, not via parenthetical author-date in-text citations like '(Booth, 1961)'. The back-matter list is titled Bibliography, not References.

expectations.description: No raw urn:mif: identifiers or f_hum_ internal IDs appear as visible prose
expectations.criteria: The strings 'urn:mif:' and 'f_hum_' must not appear as visible body text or in footnotes; internal IDs resolve to Chicago-style notes.

evals.prompt:
Produce a Chicago Notes-Bibliography humanities essay on 'memory and place in postcolonial poetry'. The finding set includes one falsified finding and one weakened finding — handle each according to the genre rules. Use footnotes and a Bibliography; no Method/Results. Write the essay to humanities-essay.md.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_post_1 | title: Postcolonial poets often reterritorialize colonial place-names to contest inherited geographies | dimension: literary-criticism | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/reterritorialize | author: Ashcroft | year: 2001
- id: urn:mif:f_post_2 | title: All postcolonial poetry uniformly rejects every European form without exception | dimension: literary-criticism | verdict: falsified | citation: https://example.com/uniform-claim | author: Blog | year: 2024
- id: urn:mif:f_post_3 | title: Diasporic memory may operate through sensory image rather than narrative, though the evidence base is contested | dimension: literary-criticism | verdict: weakened | citation: https://example.com/sensory-memory | author: Essay | year: 2019
evals.expected_output: An essay that (1) excludes the falsified uniform-rejection claim (f_post_2), (2) includes the weakened sensory-memory reading (f_post_3) with an explicit uncertainty qualifier, (3) builds on the survived reterritorialization claim (f_post_1), (4) keeps Introduction/argument/Conclusion/Bibliography with footnotes and no Method/Results.

deterministic_checks.description: Survived finding f_post_1 (reterritorialization) appears in the essay
Weakened finding f_post_3 (sensory memory) appears with annotation (weakened = include)
No raw urn:mif: identifiers appear in the prose
No internal MIF finding IDs appear in the prose
The falsified finding's source URL (example.com/uniform-claim) must not appear — falsified units are excluded, not cited (deterministic guard on the exclude-falsified rule)

expectations.description: The falsified uniform-rejection claim does NOT appear as a supported reading
expectations.criteria: The claim that all postcolonial poetry uniformly rejects every European form (Blog 2024) must not appear as a supported argument. It may be referenced only when describing the verification/exclusion policy.

expectations.description: The weakened sensory-memory reading carries an explicit uncertainty qualifier
expectations.criteria: The sensory-memory reading is presented with a qualifier such as 'contested', 'the evidence is thin', or 'tentatively' — not asserted with the confidence of the survived reterritorialization claim.

evals.prompt:
Write a Chicago Notes-Bibliography humanities essay from these findings. Thesis topic: 'the ethics of spectatorship in early documentary photography'. All three findings survived. Citations are mandatory; every claim must trace to a finding via a footnote. No Method/Results. Write the essay to humanities-essay.md.

Findings:
- id: urn:mif:f_phot_1 | title: Early documentary photographers framed poverty in ways that solicited reform yet risked aestheticizing suffering | dimension: visual-culture | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/documentary-ethics | author: Sontag | year: 1977
- id: urn:mif:f_phot_2 | title: The caption decisively shapes how a documentary image is read | dimension: visual-culture | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/caption-meaning | author: Barthes | year: 1980
- id: urn:mif:f_phot_3 | title: Spectatorship carries an ethical relation between viewer and the depicted subject | dimension: visual-culture | verdict: survived | citation: https://example.com/spectatorship-ethics | author: Azoulay | year: 2008
evals.expected_output: A complete argumentative essay with Introduction (thesis), thematic argument sections, Conclusion, and a Bibliography. Numbered footnotes carry every citation; each claim traces to a finding. No Method or Results section. No urn:mif: leakage in prose or footnotes.

deterministic_checks.description: No raw urn:mif: identifiers appear in the body text or footnotes
No internal MIF finding ID handles leak into the output body

expectations.description: Every claim traces to a supplied finding via a footnote
expectations.criteria: Each interpretive claim carries a numbered footnote citing the named author from the findings (Sontag, Barthes, Azoulay) with the source URL, and the Bibliography lists all three alphabetically.

expectations.description: The essay argues a thesis rather than reporting a method
expectations.criteria: The Introduction states a thesis about the ethics of spectatorship and the body sections advance it through interpretation; there is no Method or Results section.
